coredata.dc:title: H2O2-free preparation of K2SiF6:Mn4+ and remarkable high luminescent thermal stability induced by coating with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuelan
Zhong, Xue
Yu, Yan
Liu, Youmiao
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
Zhang, Huaxin
coredata.dc:description: A series of K2SiF6:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs (KSF:K2SiF6, Cl-GQDs: Cl containing graphene quantum dots) phosphors were prepared with a H2O2-free hydrothermal method, and the optimal sample was KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol. Enhanced effects on luminescent intensity and thermal stability inducing by coating of Cl-GQDs are observed: (a) Integrated emission intensity is 4.60 times of that of KSF:0.066Mn4+, (b) The luminescent thermal stability is greatly enhanced, which integrated emission intensities at 180 and 240 °C are 282.9 and 135.3% of that at 30 °C, respectively. The mechanisms of the above behaviors are discussed and proposed: Coordination between conjugated large π bond of GQDs and Mn4+ leads to the enhancement of emission intensity and luminescent thermal stability. Thermal stability analysis results show that the optimal samples suitable for high power WLEDs. Warm white light with low correlated color temperature and high color rendering index was produced from prototype WLEDs using the optimal sample as red emitting component.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Optical composite materials
Luminescent thermal stability
Red emitting phosphor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios on the luminescent properties
Luminescent properties at different Cl-GQDs concentration

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic digram of preparation for KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol: (a) preparation process, (b) Coating with GQDs.
XRD patterns of two samples, (i) KSF:0.066Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.066Mn4+@ Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol.
SEM images and the corresponding EDS spectra of two samples, (i) KSF: 0.066Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol: (a) and (b) SEM images, (c) and (d) EDS spectra.
PLE and PL spectra of two samples, (i) KSF:0.066Mn4+, (ii) KSF: 0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol: (a) PLE and PL spectra, (b) PL spectra, (c) Decay curves, (d) CIE chromaticity diagram.
Effect of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios (x) on the luminescent properties of KSF:xMn4+: (a) PLE, (b) PL.
PL spectra of KSF: 0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs y mg/mol.

PL performances of two samples at different temperature, (i) KSF:0.066Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol: (a), (b) PL spectra; (c) Integrated intensity curve; (d) Integrated intensity curves of stoke and anti-stoke peaks, (e) Chromaticity shift of sample (ii),(f) CIE color coordinates of sample (ii), (g) E
a curves. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Properties of prototype WLEDs fabricated by the mixing phosphores of KSF: 0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current: (a) Electroluminescence spectrum, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram and (c) Luminescence photo.

head.ce:title: H2O2-free preparation of K2SiF6:Mn4+ and remarkable high luminescent thermal stability induced by coating with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of K2SiF6:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs (KSF:K2SiF6, Cl-GQDs: Cl containing graphene quantum dots) phosphors were prepared with a H2O2-free hydrothermal method, and the optimal sample was KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol. Enhanced effects on luminescent intensity and thermal stability inducing by coating of Cl-GQDs are observed: (a) Integrated emission intensity is 4.60 times of that of KSF:0.066Mn4+, (b) The luminescent thermal stability is greatly enhanced, which integrated emission intensities at 180 and 240 °C are 282.9 and 135.3% of that at 30 °C, respectively. The mechanisms of the above behaviors are discussed and proposed: Coordination between conjugated large π bond of GQDs and Mn4+ leads to the enhancement of emission intensity and luminescent thermal stability. Thermal stability analysis results show that the optimal samples suitable for high power WLEDs. Warm white light with low correlated color temperature and high color rendering index was produced from prototype WLEDs using the optimal sample as red emitting component.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to meet ever-growing energy saving demands, development of phosphor-converted white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), that is, a new generation of solid state light source has gained momentum [1–3]. Red emitting phosphors are important components for WLEDs. The phosphors can effectively improve the color rendering index (Ra>80) and decrease the correlated color temperature (CCT<4000 K) of WLEDs making them suitable for indoor lighting [2–7]. Mn4+-doped fluoride red emitting phosphors are such red-emitting phosphors. Unfortunately, the phosphors suffer from severe degradation in emission intensity, due to thermal quenching at operating temperature of WLEDs. In addition, the luminescent intensity of the red phosphor is much weaker than that of YAG:Ce3+ yellow phosphor, because the mass ratio of the red emitting phosphor to the yellow phosphor is more than 3.5 in practical application [8]. Therefore, how to make the red emitting phosphors have both high luminescent intensity and thermal stability is a challenging task.

It has been reported that some of Mn4+-doped phosphors had negative thermal quenching behavior, resulting in improvement of their luminescent thermal stability. For example, ten Kate's group [9] reported that K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor particles coated with a thin Al2O3 layer had a negative-thermal- quenching. Ten Kate's result shows that the integrated emission intensity of the coated sample at 200 °C is about 104.6% of that value at 25 °C, respectively [9]. Liu's group [10] obtained red-emitting phosphors K2(Ge1-xSix)F6:Mn4+(x = 0.3) with negative-thermal- quenching by co-precipitation method. The result shows that the intensity at 152 °C is about 117% of that value at 25 °C [10]. Liang's group [11] reported that red-emitting phosphor Rb2TiF6:Mn4+ with high thermal-quenching resistance was synthesized via the ion exchange method under mild condition. The temperature dependent emission spectra of the Rb2TiF6:Mn4+ indicate that the phosphor shares high thermal quenching resistance, which the intensity at 150 °C is about 120% of that value at 25 °C [11]. Ye's group [12] reported that CsMoO2F3:Mn4+ with remarkable negative thermal quenching and high color stability was synthesized via simple co-precipitation method. The results show that its relative emission intensity preserves about 129.3% when the temperature rises from room temperature to 150 °C. Therefore, it is an effective method to improve the luminescent thermal stability of Mn4+-doped phosphors by using the negative thermal quenching effect.

Furthermore, numerous efforts are devoted to preparing novel Mn4+-doped fluoride red emitting phosphors via different synthesis methods [2–7]. Most of the methods [2,3] use H2O2 as starting material, which activator K2MnF6 is formed from the reaction between KMnO4 and H2O2. However, the reaction is very violent and difficult to be applied in industry. But in order to avoid the violent reaction, some methods do not use H2O2 as reducing agent [4–7]. For example, Wang's group [4] successfully prepared red-emitting phosphors K2XF6:Mn4+ (X = Ti, Si and Ge) by one-pot H2O2-free chemical co-precipitation method in low HF concentration with the addition of acetic acid glacial by using XO2 (X = Ti, Si and Ge), acetic acid glacial (99.5%), HF (40%), KMnO4 and KF as starting materials. Zhou's group [5] obtained red-emitting phosphors Na2XF6:Mn4+ (X = Si, Ge, Ti) by a simple H2O2-free co-precipitation method by using SiO2, TiO2, GeO2, NaF, HF, and NaMnO4·H2O as raw materials. Pan's group [6] reported that a red phosphor K2TiF6:Mn4+ was synthesized by H2O2-free etching Ti(OC4H9)4 in HF solution with KMnO4 and KF at room temperature for 30 min. Han's group [7] also reported that a novel K2xBa1-xTiF6:Mn4+ phosphor with high luminescent efficiency, high luminescent thermal stability and good waterproofness was synthesized through a facile H2O2-free hydrothermal method by using KCl, BaCl2·2H2O, KMnO4, H2TiF6 (50 wt%) and HF (40 wt%) as raw materials. The details results of Han's report are as follows [7]: (a) when the optimal sample (K0.070Ba0.965TiF6:0.018Mn4+) compared with the control sample (BaTiF6:0.006Mn4+), the luminescent intensity of former is increased by 6 times, the quantum efficiency is improved from 40 to 75% and the activation energy of thermal quenching is improved to 0.940 eV from 0.628 eV. (b) The waterproofness of the optimal sample is improved by 45% than traditional red phosphor K2TiF6:Mn4+ after being immersed in water for 150 min.

In summary, as far as H2O2-free methods are concerned, many achievements have been made in preparing Mn4+-doped fluoride red emitting phosphors [4–7]. In the methods, H2XF6 (X = Ti, Si and Ge) are formed from main raw materials of XO2 (X = Ti, Si and Ge), Ti(OC4H9)4 and H2TiF6, which activator K2MnF6 is obtained by oxidation of H2O by KMnO4 in HF solution. The final products are BaXF6 or A2XF6 (A = K, Na; X = Ti, Si and Ge). Although there is little report on the synthesis of Mn4+-doped fluorides with A2XF6 as starting material in H2O2-free methods, it implies that in the methods, the Mn4+-doped fluoride red emitting phosphors can also be synthesized with A2XF6 as starting material. Therefore, this paper tries to use K2SiF6 as the starting material to synthesize K2SiF6:Mn4+ by the H2O2-free method.

Due to their exceptional optical, electronic, thermal, magnetic and mechanical properties nanomaterials or quantum dots are versatile materials and have been applied in many fields [13–23]. Among of them, the carbon-based monolayer nano-sheets, such as graphene, graphite oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and GQDs have attracted lots of significant attention. Because when compared with nanoparticles [18–23], they have their own unique characteristics. The unique characteristics are as follows; high surface area, conjugated large π bonds, many uses, environment friendliness, and high thermal stability [13–17]. So, many research efforts have been performed to do related research. For examples, Zheng's group [13] found that the luminescence of Eu3+-TCs complexe (TCs: tetracyclines) was significantly enhanced by GQDs, which emission intensity ratio of Eu3+-TCs-GQDs to Eu3+-TCs is about 12.1. Zhu's group [14] reported that the luminescence of L-Tb3+ complexe (L: 1,1 ':2 ′,1 ″-terphenyl-4,4 ″-dicarboxylic acid) was obviously enhanced by GO, which emission intensity ratio of GO-L-Tb3+ to L-Tb3+ is 3.15. Zhang's group also reported [15] that the luminescence of Tb(acac)n(PMA)n complexe was greatly enhanced by GO, and emission intensity ratio of Tb(acac)n(PMA)n/GO to Tb(acac)n(PMA)n is as high as 10.5. Poddar's group [16] coated beta-NaYF4:Gd3+/Tb3+ phosphors with GQDs. The results showed that compared with the uncoated control sample, the luminescence intensity of the coated sample was 7.4 times of that of the uncoated sample. Yoo's group [17] enwrapped commercial SrBaSi2O2N2:Eu2+ phosphors with rGO. The results showed that compared with the uncoated control sample, the coated sample had long-term thermally stablily. So, the above results imply that when Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors are coated with GQDs, the GQDs will not only enhance the luminescence intensity of the phosphors, but also increase their luminescence thermal stability.

In our previous work, K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs was synthesized by combination of ion exchange method and a simple coating method [24]. As a part of our system research, in this paper, based on H2O2-free hydrothermal method, an efficient strategy is built to form K2SiF6:Mn4+@Cl-GQDs hybrid composite materials. Some novel coating effects of Cl-GQDs were observed, which both the luminescence intensity and luminescence thermal stability were greatly enhanced.

Cl-contained graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) were purchased from the Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd., China, and used without further purification. In addition, most chemicals were reagent-grade pure and purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. XRD was performed at a scanning rate of 5°/min from 5 to 70° for 2θ at room temperature by using a Rigaku D/max 2500 V diffractometer equipped with a graphite monochromator by utilizing monochromatic CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). The elemental contents of sample were determined by using a PerkinElmer Optima 3100 R L inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES). First, samples were dissolved in 10 mL of 1:1 HCl aqueous solution contained several drops of H2O2 (30%). Second, the solution was diluted to 100 mL with deionized water. Third, the metal elemental contents in the solution were examined with ICP-AES. The morphologies of the samples were examined by Hitachi S-3400 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with an attached energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). Samples were mounted on an aluminum slice coated with Au. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission (PL) spectra were recorded at room temperature by a Horiba Fluoro Max-4-R F6000 spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source. The photoluminescence quantum yield and the luminescence decay curve were obtained from an Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. TG data were recorded with a NETZSCH STA 409 PC/PG thermogravimetric analyzer. Purity nitrogen gas (99.999%) was used as protective atmosphere with a flow rate of 20 mL/min. Sample powders of 7 ± 0.1 mg mass were used for the experiments with heating rates of 10 °C/min. The samples were loaded without pressing into a platinum crucible. The performance of WLEDs was measured using an auto temperatured LED opto-electronic analyzer (LATA-1000, Everfine).

A H2O2-free hydrothermal method is used to obtain K2SiF6:Mn4+ . First,1.00 g kF·2H2O, 0.17 g (1.1 mmol) KMnO4 and 10 mL HF (40%) are put in a plastic beaker, and stirred 10 min with magnetic stirrers to get a purplish red solution. Second, 2.20 g (10 mmol) powders of K2SiF6 (x = molar ratio of Mn/Si = 0.11) are added into the solution, stirred for 15 min. Third, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 10 h. Then, the autoclave was taken out of the oven and cooled naturally to room temperature to obtain a mixture (I). Finally, the primrose yellow crystals were collected, filtered and washed with acetone several times and dried at 80 °C for 2 h to yield sample (i), K2SiF6:xMn4+(x = 0.11)(KSF: xMn4+, x = 0.11). Synthetic procedures for KSF:xMn4+ samples with x = 0.01–0.15 are as the same as that of KSF:xMn4+(x = 0.11).

First, 80 μl Cl-GQDs (1.00 mg/mL) and 5 mL HF (40%) are put in a plastic beaker, and stirred 10 min with magnetic stirrers to get a solution. Second, the solution was added into the mixture (I) at room temperature, stirred for 15 min. Third, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 3 h. Then, the autoclave was taken out of the oven and cooled naturally to room temperature. Finally, the primrose yellow crystals were collected, filtered and washed with acetone several times and dried at 80 °C for 2 h to yield sample (ii), K2SiF6:xMn4+@ Cl-GQDs8 mg/mol (x = 0.11) (KSF:xMn4+@Cl-GQDs8 mg/mol, x = 0.11). Synthetic procedures for KSF: xMn4+@Cl-GQDs y mg/mol (x = 0.11) samples with y = 2.0–12.0 mg/mol are as the same as that of KSF:xMn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol (x = 0.11). Schematic digram of preparation for KSF:xMn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol (x = 0.11) is shown in Fig. 1 .

The ICP elemental analysis data of the sample (i) show that the mass percentages of K, Si and Mn are 35.20, 11.81, and 1.64%, respectively. In other words, the molar ratio of K:Si:Mn in the sample (i) is 2.000:0.9341:0.0663. So, the corresponding molecular formulas of the samples (i)-(ii) are: (i) K2SiF6:0.066Mn4+ (KSF:0.066Mn4+), (ii) K2SiF6:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs8 mg/mol (KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs8 mg/mol). The results show that about 64.54% of Mn is doped into KSF crystals of the sample (i).

The chemical process for synthesizing the sample (i) from reactants is represented by the following formulas.
(1) 4KMnO4 + 4 KF + 20HF = 4K2MnF6 + 10H2O + 3O2
(2) 4KMnO4+12HF = 4MnF2+6H2O +5O2 + 4 KF
(3)
xK2MnF6 + (1-x)K2SiF6 = K2Si1-x F6:xMn4+

Formulas (1) and (2) are chemical reactions in which H2O as reducing agent is oxidized by KMnO4 in acid condition. Formula (3) is the reaction for the formation of KSF:xMn4+. MnF2 formed from Formula (2) is soluble and remained in the filtrate. Therefore, the doping rate of Mn is only 64.54%, which is supported by similar result from Lv et al.‘s report [6].

The XRD patterns of two samples: (i)KSF:0.066Mn4+, (ii)KSF:0.066Mn4+ @Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol are shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2, all the diffraction peaks of the two samples are indexed to obtain the following lattice parameters: (i) a = b = c = 0.813603 (4) nm, α = β = γ = 90° (ii) for sample (i); (ii) a = b = c = 0.813608 (6) nm, α = β = γ = 90° for sample (ii). The indexed results are in agreement with the standard data of PDF#75–0694 (cubic K2SiF6 phase with space group Fm
3 ‾
m (225) and lattice parameters of a = b = c = 0.8134 nm,α = β = γ = 90°). Fig. 2 shows that there are no other impurities in the two samples, indicating that the two samples are pure phase samples. Furthermore, no peak of Cl-GQDs appears in Fig. 2. Because the mass percentage of Cl-GQDs in the sample (ii) is only about 0.0036%, and the content of Cl-GQDs is too small to produce diffraction peak.

Fig. 3 shows SEM images and the EDS spectra of samples, (i) KSF:0.066Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol. Fig. 3a–b shows that the two samples are irregular nubbly crystals with a size of about 2–5 μm. There are a few of differences in crystal morphologies of the samples (i) and (ii), which the surface of the latter is slightly smoother than that of the former. Fig. 3c–d shows that the sample (i) has elements of K, Si, Mn and F, while elements of the sample (ii) are K, Si, Mn, F and C. The presence of C element in Fig. 3d suggests that Cl-GQDs has been successfully coated on the surface of K2SiF6:Mn4+. But, no peak of Cl is observed in Fig. 3d, indicating that Cl of Cl-GQDs may be replaced by F during the preparation process. Mondal et al. [22,23] reported that some functional films could be easily prepared by spin-coated method with nanoparticles (or sol-gel) as starting materials at relatively low temperature (200–250 °C). In this work, the target product containing Cl-GQDs coating layer can be obtained by hydrothermal method at 120 °C using Cl-GQDs as raw material. The reason why the temperature required by the method of this work is lower is that compared with nanoparticles, Cl-GQDs are sheet quantum dots with smaller size, and have larger specific surface area, so they are easier to adsorb on the surface of the particles to form a coating layer.

Fig. 4 shows room temperature fluorescent properties of the samples (i)(KSF:0.066Mn4+) and (ii)(KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol). It can be seen from Fig. 4 that peak positions in PLE and PL bands of the two samples have no difference. Fig. 4a shows that the PLE bands of the two samples have two intense broad excitation peaks centered at ~360 and ~467 nm, which belong to the spin-allowed 4A2g→4T1g and 4A2g→4T2g transitions of Mn4+, respectively [25–27]. Fig. 4b shows that the PL spectra of the samples (i) and (ii) consists of several sharp emission peaks around ~630 nm, corresponding to the spin-forbidden 2Eg → 4A2 transitions of Mn4+ [25–27].

From Fig. 4, it can be found that effects of coating Cl-GQDs are as follows: (a) The PLE and integrated PL intensities of the sample (ii) are greatly enhanced, which integrated PL intensity is 4.60 times of that of the sample (i). The enhanced result is much better than that of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs obtained via cation exchange method, which integrated PL intensity of the coated sample is about 1.32 times of that of uncoated sample [24], showing that different enhanced effect can be obtained by different preparation methods. (b) The sample (i) has no zero phonon line (ZPL), which the sample (ii) has a weak ZPL. It can be learned from the literature that there is no observable ZPL in PL spectrum of K2SiF6: Mn4+ [28,29]. ZPL is depended on local crystal field environments of Mn4+ in the host. That is that the lower symmetry of the Mn4+ sites in the host lattice is, the stronger ZPL is [30]. Furthermore, Mn4+ in the host with crystal structures of Fm
3 ‾
m does not show observable ZPL peak [31,32]. From the result of Fig. 2, it can be known that the space group of the sample (ii) is Fm
3 ‾
m, suggesting it should have no observable ZPL peak. But, actually, Fig. 4b shows that there is an observable ZPL in the PL spectrum of the sample (ii). So, the observable ZPL of the sample (ii) suggests that there is interaction between Mn4+and GQDs, and results in distortion of the local crystal field.

The decay curves of the samples (i) and (ii) are shown in Fig. 4c. They can be fitted by a single-exponential function with a constant term, and the results are as follows: τ (i) = 6.94 and τ (ii) = 7.16 ms, respectively. Results of Fig. 4c show that the lifetime of the sample (ii) increases induced by coating with Cl-GQDs.

The surface of Cl-GQDs has conjugated large π bond, so it can be used as electron donor. Theoretically, the process of energy transfer will affect the lifetime of fluorescence. The details are that the lifetime of the Cl-GQDs donor is reduced while that of the KSF:Mn4+ acceptor is increased [33]. Thus, the results of Fig. 4c show that there is energy transfer from the Cl-GQDs to KSF:Mn4+, which is agreement with our previously results [24].

Internal QY (QYi) of samples were measured by using Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere. Eq. (4) was used to calculate the QYi [34]:
(4)
QY i
=
∫
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
b l a n k
−
∫
E
s a m p l e
where L emission is the emission spectrum of the sample, E sample is the spectrum of the light used to excite the sample, and E blank is the spectrum of the light used for excitation with only the powder vessel (without the sample) in sphere. The QYi of the samples (i) and (ii) are calculated to be 69.65 and 98.93%, which the QYi of the samples (ii) is higher than that of K2SiF6:Mn4+ (92%) [35]. The results show that coating of Cl-GQDs can increase the QY of the samples.

Based on the emission spectrum data of the samples (i) and (ii) (Fig. 4b), the chromaticity coordinates of the two samples are determined with “CIE1931”. The chromaticity coordinate results of the two samples are shown in Fig. 4d, and they are: (a) x = 0.6792 and y = 0.3206 for the sample (i), (b) x = 0.6880 and y = 0.3119 for the sample (ii). Fig. 4d shows color of the sample (ii) is redder than that of the sample (i). Moreover, Eq. (5) [36–38] is used to calculate the color purities of the two samples.
(5)
Cp =
(
x −
x i
)
2
+
(
y −
y i
)
2
(
x d
−
x i
)
2
+
(
y d
−
y i
)
2
× 100 %

Here, (x, y) are the color coordinates of the sample, that is (a) x = 0.6792 and y = 0.3206 for the sample (i), (b) x = 0.6880 and y = 0.3119 for the sample (ii); (x
i
, y
i ) are the CIE of an equal-energy illuminant with a value of (0.33, 0.33), and (x
d
, y
d ) are the chromaticity coordinates of the light source dominant wavelength (For 467 nm excitation, x
d = 0.1314, y
d = 0.0459). The color purities of the two samples are: (a) C
p = 98.89% for the sample (i), (b) C
p = 99.97% for the sample (ii), indicating that the color purity of the sample can be improved by coating of Cl-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios on the luminescent properties
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios on the luminescent properties is shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a–b shows that both the influences of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios on PL and PLE intensities are parabola curves. First, the PLE and PL intensities of the samples is increased with the increase of KMnO4/K2SiF6 molar ratios, and achieve a max value at x = 11%, then decline when x is larger 11%, due to concentration quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties at different Cl-GQDs concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Based on KSF:0.066Mn4+ (x = 0.11), the luminescent properties at different Cl-GQDs concentration was studied, and shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6 shows that the PL intensity curve with different Cl-GQDs concentration is a parabola with a downward opening. First, the intensities are enhanced with the increase of Cl-GQDs concentration, and achieve a max value at y = 8 mg/mol, then are decreased when y exceeds 8 mg/mol, due to concentration quenching. In addition, Fig. 6 also shows that Cl-GQDs only affect the intensity of PL spectra, not the shape of PL spectra.

Luminescent thermal performances of the samples (i) and (ii) at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a–b shows that integrated PL intensities of the two samples are strongly influenced by temperature, which PL peaks are broadened with temperature increasing, but positions of emission peaks do not shift with the increase of temperature. Fig. 7c shows curves of integrated PL intensities (580–680 nm) of the two samples at different temperatures. From Fig. 7c, it can be seen that the curves of the two samples are all parabola curves. The similarities of the two curves are as follows: First, the integrated PL intensities of the two samples are enhanced with the increase of temperatures, and reach a max, then reduce when the temperature is over the max temperatures. The max temperatures for samples (i) and (ii) are 90 and 180 °C, respectively, which the latter is much higher that the former. Fig. 7c also shows that after five cycles of testing (30–240 °C), the luminescent intensities remain unchanged, indicating that luminescent thermal stability and chemical thermal stability of the two samples are high.

The differences between the two curves are also obvious: For the sample (ii), the integrated PL intensities at 90, 180 and 240 °C are 168.2, 282.9 and 135.4% of that value at 30 °C, respectively. While, for the sample (i), they are only 107.5, 78.9 and 30.3 of that value at 30 °C, respectively. The results of Fig. 7c show that luminescent thermal stability of the sample (ii) is much higher than that of the sample (i). It is reported that for some K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphors synthesized from co-precipitation method [39,40], their integrated intensities at 120 and 180 °C are about 1.12 and 0.75 times of that value at 25 °C [39]. So, the results of the sample (i) are slightly worse than that of the above K2SiF6:Mn4+ [39].
A crucial parameter for phosphors to be used for high-power WLEDs applications is luminescent thermal stability, due to the operating temperatures of WLEDs chip being over 150 °C [8].

Some authors reported that several Mn4+-doped phosphors had negative thermal quenching behavior [8,24,41,42]. The details are as follows: (a) For α-K3AlF6:Mn4+ prepared via a cation-exchange method, its integrated emission intensities at 125 °C and at 200 °C are about 2.2 and 1.18 times of that value at 25 °C [8]. (b) For K2SiF6:xMn4+ with negative-thermal-quenching obtained by a HF-free hydrothermal method, its integrated intensities at 167 and 207 °C are about 1.10 and 1.07 times of that value at 25 °C [41]. (c) For K2TiF6:Mn4+ synthesized by a two-step wet-chemical method at a low temperature of −16 °C, its integrated intensities at 150 and 202 °C are about 1.67 and 1.89 times of that value at 25 °C [42]. In addition, for K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs obtained via cation exchange method and room temperature coating method in our previous work, the intensities at 120 and 180 °C are about 157 and 126% of that value at 25 °C [24].

In comparison, the negative-thermal-quenching effect of the sample (ii) is much larger than those of the above four samples. So, coating with Cl-GQDs or different coating method will lead to having different luminescent thermal performances. Therefore, when applied for WLEDs, the sample (ii) will have enough red emission intensity at 180 °C.

It can be seen from the results of Fig. 7c that the coating of Cl-GQDs can effectively improve the luminescent thermal stability of KSF:Mn4+. The reason why the sample (ii) has such high luminescent thermal stability may be attributed to the promotion of Cl-GQDs. There is evidence that both the nonradiative and radiative transition probabilities of Mn4+ gradually increase with increasing temperature [8,43]. Therefore, mechanism of the negative-thermal-quenching for the sample (ii) may be described as follows: In a given temperature range, under promotion of the Cl-GQDs donor with conjugated large π bond, the radiative transition probability will increase much faster than that of the nonradiative with increase of temperature. Then, emission intensity is enhanced with increase of temperature.

Fig. 7d shows that integrated intensities of Stokes and anti-Stokes peaks of samples (i) and (ii) change at different temperature. It can be seen from Fig. 7d that with the increase of temperature, curves of the intensities of anti-Stokes transitions (W
a ) and the intensities of Stokes transitions (W s) show a rising trend, but the rising trend of W
a curve is larger than that of W s curve. The curves reach their tops when the temperatures are 90 and 180 °C for samples (i) and (ii), respectively. These negative thermal quenching phenomena are in according with Eqs. (6) and (7) [44]:
(6)
W s
( T )
= D ·
exp
(
ℏ ω
k T
)
exp
(
ℏ ω
k T
)
− 1
(7)
W a
( T )
= D ·
1
exp
(
ℏ ω
k T
)
− 1
where D is the proportional coefficient, ħω is the energy of the coupled vibronic mode, T is temperature and k is the Boltzmann constant. The equations indicate that the W a and W s would increase with the rise of temperature. Furthermore, the equations also show that the W a will rise more rapidly than W
s with the increasing temperature. The integrated emission intensities of the samples (Fig. 7c) also reach the tops at the same temperature, which are about 107.5 and 282.9% of those initial values for the two samples, respectively. Meanwhile, the emission intensity of the sample (ii) at 240 °C (135.4%) is still higher than that initial value.

Eqs. (6) and (7) suggest that the major mechanism of the negative-thermal quenching effect for the two samples might be attributed to phonon-induced radiative transition probability increasing faster than that of nonradiative transition probability. In other words, part of heat energy is converted into light radiation energy by the phonon-induced.

Furthermore, because the Cl-GQDs has conjugated large π bond, so it can be used as electron donor to coordinate with Mn4+ ions. Under promotion of the coordinating between Cl-GQDs and Mn4+ ions, the negative-thermal quenching effect of the sample (ii) is much larger than that of the sample (i).

Eq. (8) [45–48] are used to calculate the chromaticity shifts (ΔE) of the sample (ii) at different temperature:
(8)
Δ E =
(
u t ′
−
u 0 ′
)
2
+
(
v t ′
−
v 0 ′
)
2
+
(
w t ′
−
w 0 ′
)
2
where u’ = 4x/(3–2x + 12y), v’ = 9y/(3–2x + 12y) and w’ = 1- u’ – v’. u’ and v’ are the chromaticity coordinates in v’ and u’ uniform color space, x and y are the chromaticity coordinates in the CIE 1931 color space, and 0 and t are the chromaticity shift at 300 K and a given temperature.

Fig. 7e shows that the dependence of ΔE on temperature for the sample (ii) (KSF:0.066Mn4+@Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol) is a nonlinear curve, which ΔE of the sample (ii) increases with the temperature increasing. According to the curve, values of ΔE at 150, 175 and 200 °C are 10.85 × 10−3, 13.60 × 10−3 and 17.00 × 10−3, respectively. The ΔE of the sample (ii) at 175 °C is better than that of CSASNE (44 × 10−3 at 175 °C) [48], showing that it can be satisfy the requirement of WLEDs.

Fig. 7f shows CIE color coordinates of the sample (ii) at different temperatures. It can be seen from Fig. 7f that there is a slight shift of color coordinates in the red region, which may be attributed to the slight broadening of the sharp red emission peaks. But, the result shows that the sample (ii) has good color stability.

The activation energy (E
a ) of the sample (ii) for thermal quenching is also explored. Eq. (9) is applied to determine the E
a . Eq. (10) can be obtained from Eq. (9) [45–48]:
(9)
I T
=
I 0
1 + A
exp
(
−
E α
k B
T
)
(10)
L n
(
I 0
I T
− 1
)
= L n A −
E α
K B
T
where I
0 and I
T are the initial emission intensity, and the intensity at different temperatures, respectively. E
α is activation energy of thermal quenching, A is a constant, and k
B is the Boltzmann constant (8.617 × 10−5 eV/K). The E
α can be obtained from slope of a line (Eq. (10)) as shown in Fig. 7g. From the slopes of two lines in Fig. 7g, the E
α values for the samples (i) and (iii) are determined to be 0.5757 and 1.3286 eV, respectively. It can be seen that E
α is effectively increased by coating with Cl-GQDs.

TG curve of the sample (ii) is shown in Fig. 8 . The TG curve below 1000 °C has two steps with different mass loss. The mass loss begins at about 80 °C. Then, at 300 °C, the mass loss is only 0.033%, which belongs to the loss of adsorbed water. The result indicates that the sample has high chemical thermal stability.

Fig. 9 shows properties of prototype WLEDs, which is composed with the mixing phosphors of the sample (ii) and yellow emitting YAG:Ce3+ with blue emitting InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current. Spectrum of the WLEDs is shown in Fig. 9a. The chromaticity coordinates of the WLEDs are calculated by using spectrum data of Fig. 9 a, the results are: x = 0.3555 and y = 0.3065 (Fig. 9b), indicating that the WLEDs has warm white light (CCT = 4316 K). Furthermore, other performances of prototype WLEDs are luminescent efficiency = 102 l m W−1, R
a = 86.2 and R
9 = 80.1. Fig. 9c also shows that the WLEDs emit warm white light. Thus, the sample (ii) can be applied to improve the performance of WLEDs for indoor lighting.

In summary, a series of KSF:0.066Mn4+ @Cl-GQDs phosphors were synthesized by using a H2O2-free hydrothermal method, and the optimal sample was KSF:0.066Mn4+ @Cl-GQDs 8 mg/mol. Dual enhanced effects on luminescent intensity and thermal stability inducing by coating of Cl-GQDs for the optimal sample are observed: (a) The integrated PL intensity is 4.60 times of that of KSF: 0.066Mn4+, (b) The luminescent thermal stability is greatly enhanced, which the integrated PL intensities at 180 and 240 °C are 282.9 and 135.4% of that at 30 °C. Thermal stability analysis results show that the optimal sample exhibits unusual luminescent thermal stability as well as color stability under WLEDs working temperature, which imply that the sample is suitable for high power WLEDs. Warm white light with luminescence efficiency = 102 l m W−1, CCT = 4316 K, R
a = 86.2 and R
9 = 80.1 was produced from prototype WLEDs using the mixing phosphors of the optimal sample and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current, indicating the sample can be applied for blue light-based warm WLEDs.

Yuelan Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xue Zhong: Investigation. Yan Yu: Investigation. Youmiao Liu: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Huaxin Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Visualization.
